title: Daily Market Digest
subtitle: March 20, 2026: Markets Retreat Amid Geopolitical Tensions and Inflation Concerns

items.headline: US Equities Close Lower as Geopolitical Worries and Rising Yields Dampen Sentiment
items.summary: US stock markets closed lower on Friday after a choppy session, with all three major indices ending in negative territory. Investor pessimism surged, with 52% of respondents to the latest AAII survey holding a bearish outlook for the next six months, a notable increase fueled by the ongoing conflict in the Middle East. Rising Treasury yields, which increase borrowing costs, also weighed on market sentiment. Despite early pre-market futures showing slight gains on eased geopolitical worries, the overall mood remained cautious amid mixed economic data and persistent inflation concerns.

items.statContext: Down 0.3% to its lowest close in four months, breaking below its 200-day moving average.
Slipped 0.3% due to weak performance from corporate bigwigs, also closing below its 200-day moving average.
Fell 203.72 points (-0.4%), marking its lowest close for 2026 and ending below its 200-day moving average.

items.headline: Micron Technology (MU)
items.summary: Shares tumbled 3.9% after its third-quarter fiscal 2026 guidance failed to impress the market.

items.headline: Super Micro Computer (SMCI)
items.summary: Plunged 28.2% after US authorities accused a senior executive and two affiliates of conspiring to smuggle advanced Nvidia-based servers to China.

items.headline: U Power Limited (UCAR)
items.summary: Dived 65.08% after announcing and pricing a heavily dilutive $6 million public offering of units.

items.headline: FedEx (FDX)
items.summary: Rose 2.8% after reporting quarterly earnings that exceeded analysts' expectations.

items.headline: Dell Technologies (DELL)
items.summary: Showed early gains, up +6.28, in a generally down market.

items.headline: S&P 500 Sectors Universally Negative; Software and Auto Industries Lagging
items.summary: All 11 sectors of the broad-market S&P 500 ended in negative territory today. Consumer Discretionary (XLY) slipped 2.6%, Health Care (XLV) by 2.5%, and Consumer Staples (XLP) by 2.1%. The software industry continues to be the worst-performing sector, with 97% of its companies down 20% or more, followed by automobiles (75%) and media & entertainment (63%). In contrast, the Energy and Utilities sectors have shown resilience, with no energy stocks experiencing a 20% drawdown and only 6% of utility stocks doing so. Energy stocks had surged 25% year-to-date as of early March, benefiting from rising oil prices amid geopolitical tensions.

items.headline: Fed Holds Rates Steady, Inflation Concerns Persist Amid Geopolitical Conflict
items.summary: The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) maintained the federal funds rate at the 3.5%-3.75% range, though one governor dissented, favoring a 25-basis-point cut. The committee acknowledged robust economic growth but noted that inflation has exceeded expectations, with heightened uncertainty due to the ongoing war in Iran impacting energy prices. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell warned that surging energy costs could pressure inflation expectations, leading traders to anticipate no rate cuts for the remainder of 2026. Economic data released today was mixed; the Philadelphia Fed Index surged to 18.1 in March, its highest level in 2026, significantly above estimates. However, Leading Indicators fell 0.1% month-over-month in January, and wholesale inventories also decreased by 0.5%. Job openings in January slightly increased to 6.9 million.

items.headline: Middle East Conflict Fuels Inflationary Pressure, Treasury Yields Climb
items.summary: The ongoing Middle East conflict between the U.S.-Israel and Iran continues to be a dominant macro trend, driving crude oil and natural gas prices higher and fueling concerns about inflationary pressure. Brent crude remained elevated, trading around $108-$110 per barrel. In fixed-income markets, the yield on the 10-year US Treasury climbed to 4.32%, up from 4.25% late Thursday, reflecting concerns that prolonged disruptions to energy supply could push inflation higher and lead to increased borrowing costs. The S&P 500 also broke its 200-day moving average after a 214-day streak, which some analysts view as a bearish sign. Today's 'quadruple witching' event, involving the simultaneous expiration of trillions in derivatives, was expected to amplify intraday market swings.
